name: Soufflé
summary: Compiles Datalog programs to efficient C++ for static analysis. The IR here is the Datalog program itself: rules declare what facts are derivable, and the engine derives all consequences. Pointer analysis, type inference, security analysis, and compiler optimisations have been encoded as Datalog programs over Soufflé.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Soufflé compiles Datalog programs to efficient C++ for static analysis. The "IR" here is *the Datalog program itself*: rules declare what facts are derivable, and the engine derives all consequences. Pointer analysis, type inference, security analysis, and compiler optimizations have all been encoded as Datalog programs over Soufflé.

sections.title: Datalog as program representation
sections.content:
Soufflé is the production-grade Datalog engine for compiler-related uses (the academic ancestor is Bddbddb). The contribution is showing that program analysis can be expressed declaratively as Datalog rules over a relational schema, and that the engine can derive consequences faster than hand-written analyses.

The lesson generalises: when the analysis is fundamentally about deriving facts from facts (e.g., "may-alias" from points-to information, "reachable" from call-graph edges, "tainted" from source/sink relationships), expressing the analysis as a Datalog program lets the engine optimise the deduction work — index choice, evaluation order, magic-set rewriting — without rewriting the analysis logic itself. Rust's next-generation borrow checker (Polonius) is the highest-profile example — it reformulates borrow-checking as Datalog rules.
